listings.dealer: Corsinos Service Station and Repair Inc

top10.introHtml: Ranked by the cheap fun second car lens: working top, clean history, reliable model family, and manual-friendly. Mileage is flexible — a well-maintained Miata or Z3 at 130K beats a neglected anything at 40K.

picks.whyGood: The E46 325Ci is the sweet spot of BMW convertibles — inline-6, bulletproof at this mileage, and the body-style is timeless. 60K miles is genuinely low for a 2001, and a Great Deal badge at $10K from Mancini (a known local independent) is legit. No accidents, clean title. The soft top on these is simple and durable.
picks.whatToVerify: Inspect the soft top mechanism and headliner for water stains or mildew. Check the rear window delamination (common on E46 cabrios). Oil leaks at the valve cover and coolant expansion tank (plastic fails). Ask for timing chain service history. Operate the top through a full open/close cycle.
picks.bottomLine: Best all-around pick: reliable inline-6, low miles, Great Deal, 15 miles away.

picks.whyGood: Only 9 miles away in West Chester — the closest quality convertible in the batch. The '97 Z3 uses the M44 1.9L four-cylinder, which is light, willing, and extremely reliable past 150K when maintained. 69K is excellent. No accidents. Dougherty is a well-regarded small independent shop in the area that actually does BMW work.
picks.whatToVerify: Soft top condition (rear plastic window and seal around the windshield header). Check for rust on the trunk lid lower edge and under the front bumper. Confirm the soft top motor works smoothly. Ask if the cooling system (water pump, thermostat) has been serviced.
picks.bottomLine: Closest good car to Media — a Z3 roadster at $9,995 from a shop that knows BMWs.

picks.whyGood: The 2000 model year Z3 got the M52TU 2.3L inline-6 — a major step up from the 1.9L four. Same great chassis, now with smooth six-cylinder power. At $7,940 this is the cheapest inline-6 Z3 in the region with clean history. Private seller listing (no dealer markup). No accidents.
picks.whatToVerify: Verify soft top operation and rear window clarity. Check for rear subframe rust (more relevant on earlier Z3s but worth a look). Cooling system history — the M52 liked to crack its plastic coolant hose fittings. Confirm VANOS (variable valve timing) is smooth with no rattle at startup.
picks.bottomLine: Cheapest fun inline-6 convertible in the region — hard to beat at $7,940.

picks.whyGood: The GXP is the turbocharged Solstice — 260 hp from the LNF 2.0T, which is a proper driver's car engine. Only 51K miles is unusually low for a 2007. The Solstice GXP is widely considered the most fun American sports car at this price point. Well-balanced, rear-wheel drive, light.
picks.whatToVerify: Soft top seals (the Solstice top is basic but functional — check for leaks at the side windows). LNF turbo: confirm no coolant in oil, check intercooler connections. Manual trans (if equipped): test third-gear synchro. Check for any rust on rocker panels — these can be spotty. Operate the top fully.
picks.bottomLine: Turbocharged, RWD, 51K miles — the most bang-for-buck fun car on this list.

picks.whyGood: The R170 SLK 230 with the supercharged 2.3L is a tight, well-built roadster. Retractable hardtop (no fabric to rot). At $7,495 with 68K miles and a Great Deal badge, this is one of the cheapest fun cars near Media. Clean title, no accidents. Only 10 miles away in West Chester.
picks.whatToVerify: Hardtop mechanism: run through a complete open/close cycle and listen for hydraulic pump noise or hesitation. Check hydraulic fluid level. Common issues: head gasket on higher-mileage cars, timing chain wear. Inspect the interior for any signs of water intrusion around the top seal.
picks.bottomLine: Hardtop convertible at $7,495 with clean history — the best value per mile in the batch.

picks.whyGood: The 986 Boxster is the driver's car of this list — mid-engine, perfectly balanced, and endlessly satisfying. 74K miles on a 2001 is moderate. Private seller, no accidents. At $11,250 it's priced fair for a clean example. The 986 with the 2.7L actually has better IMS bearing longevity than the 2.5L cars.
picks.whatToVerify: CRITICAL: IMS (intermediate shaft) bearing — this is the 986's known weak point. Ask for documentation of IMS replacement or budget ~$2,000 for a preventive fix. Check for RMS (rear main seal) oil leak. Run compression on both banks. Inspect soft top hydraulics and rear window.
picks.bottomLine: Best driving experience on the list — budget extra for IMS inspection/replacement as insurance.

picks.whyGood: The ZZW30 MR2 Spyder is a cult classic — mid-engine, 2,000 lbs, the 1ZZ-FE is nearly bulletproof, and it's increasingly rare at any price. Private seller, no accidents, 74K miles. At $9,250 this is an excellent price for an MR2 in the area. Manual only — these were always manual.
picks.whatToVerify: Soft top: the MR2 soft top is simple but check the rear glass and weatherstripping. No significant model-specific mechanical gotchas — the 1ZZ is very reliable. Check for rear suspension corrosion (these were made to be light, not rust-proof). Inspect for oil consumption (some 1ZZ engines did use oil).
picks.bottomLine: Rare, fun, and appreciating — the MR2 Spyder is the sleeper pick on this list.

picks.whyGood: A second MR2 Spyder in the area, this one a year newer with only 56K miles — one of the lowest-mileage examples you'll find at any price. Private seller, no accidents. The lower mileage and newer year justify the $3K premium over the rank-7 car. Two MR2s in one refresh is unusual.
picks.whatToVerify: Same checks as rank 7: soft top operation, 1ZZ oil consumption, rear suspension corrosion. Confirm the SMT (sequential manual transmission) shifts cleanly if equipped — the SMT can be finicky. If manual, straightforward.
picks.bottomLine: 56K miles on an MR2 Spyder is remarkable — a true collector-grade driver's car.

picks.whyGood: The 135i is a unicorn in this budget: a turbocharged 3.0L inline-6 (N55) in a small, light, rear-wheel-drive body. 101K on an N55 is well within its service life. Good Deal rating at $11,200. The 1-series convertible is a genuine driver's car that feels nothing like the average drop-top.
picks.whatToVerify: N55 engine: check for boost/charge pipe leaks (common, cheap fix). Soft top: full open/close cycle. Check for oil leaks at the valve cover and rear main seal. HPFP (high-pressure fuel pump) recall — confirm it's been done. Watermarks on interior headliner.
picks.bottomLine: Turbocharged inline-6 drop-top under $12K — the sports car driver's pick for this budget.

picks.whyGood: The 128i's N52 naturally-aspirated inline-6 is famously reliable — no turbo complexity, great throttle response, bulletproof past 200K with normal maintenance. 75K miles at $11,250 is fair value. Willow Grove is only 23 miles out. Pairs the fun 1-series chassis with the bulletproof engine.
picks.whatToVerify: Soft top operation (full cycle). N52 engine: check for VANOS rattle at startup, cooling system (water pump and thermostat). Look for oil leaks at valve cover. Inspect the soft top rear window for delamination — common on older 1-series cabrios. Check the roof drain tubes.
picks.bottomLine: Naturally aspirated inline-6 with 75K miles — the low-drama, long-life choice.

mustangs.introHtml: All Ford Mustang convertibles in the search area, ranked GT/V8 first. Two SN-95 GT survivors with under 60K miles are the standouts.

picks.whyGood: A 1994 GT with only 44K miles is genuinely rare — this is SN-95 generation, 5.0L V8 (GT spec), private seller, clean title, no accidents. 44K is a collector-level mileage count. The SN-95 GT is the classic Fox-derived muscle convertible and this one is priced right.
picks.whatToVerify: SN-95 convertible: inspect the top-mount-to-rocker seam for frame rust (this is the known weak point on these cars — water traps there). Check soft top: rear window and latches. T-5 transmission (if 5-speed): slow-shift into 3rd to test synchros. Check the 5.0L for head gasket history and exhaust manifold cracks.
picks.bottomLine: 44K miles on a 5.0 GT convertible — an outlier find that should be inspected quickly.

picks.whyGood: The last year of the 5.0L V8 before Ford switched to the 4.6L modular. GT trim, 55K miles, no accidents, dealer-sold. The '95 GT is the purist's choice in the SN-95 generation — classic muscle car lineage with a V8 that responds well to modifications if desired.
picks.whatToVerify: Same SN-95 checks as rank 1: rocker seam rust, T-5 third gear, 5.0 head gasket. Ask dealer for any service records. Inspect soft top seals — look for water staining on rear seat and trunk carpet as a leak indicator.
picks.bottomLine: The final 5.0 GT year at 55K miles — nearly as rare as the rank-1 car with a dealer safety net.

picks.whyGood: The S197 V6 Mustang makes 305 hp from the Cyclone 3.7L — genuinely quick and much more fuel-efficient than the GT. 64K miles is low for a 2013. Clean title, no accidents. Further out at 42 miles but well priced at $10,499.
picks.whatToVerify: S197 convertibles: check the rear seat area for water staining from top seal failure. Inspect the 6R80 automatic or MT82 manual transmission. Test the power top through a full cycle. Check for any DTC codes — the 3.7 is very reliable but worth scanning.
picks.bottomLine: Modern muscle with 305 hp, low miles, and clean history — the practical Mustang pick.

picks.whyGood: Only 9 miles away in West Chester — the closest Mustang convertible in the batch. 96K miles on a 3.7 V6 is early middle age. Fair Deal pricing at $11,459. Same 305 hp Cyclone engine as the rank-3 car. Convenience and proximity make this a strong candidate for a quick look.
picks.whatToVerify: Same S197 checks: water ingress in rear seat area, power top operation, scan for codes. The 96K mileage is fine but check service history for transmission fluid and spark plugs.
picks.bottomLine: 9 miles away and ready to test drive — the most convenient Mustang option.

picks.whyGood: Premium trim adds leather, Sync, and upgraded audio. 79K miles is reasonable at this price. The 2014 is the last year of the S197 before the S550 redesign — a good-looking car with a solid dealer support network. Priced toward the top of budget but it's a nicer interior than the base cars.
picks.whatToVerify: Same S197 checks. The Premium trim added a MyColor gauge cluster — confirm all electronics work. Test the sync system. Power top full cycle. Check for the known taillight condensation issue on S197 cars.
picks.bottomLine: Best-equipped Mustang on the list — worth the premium if interior quality matters.
